articles.title: Stunning fossil discovery challenges the origins of animal life
articles.summary: Scientists revisiting mysterious 540-million-year-old microfossils from Brazil have overturned a major idea about early animal life. What were once thought to be trails left behind by tiny worm-like creatures are now believed to be fossilized communities of bacteria and algae — some with remarkably preserved cells and organic material still intact.
articles.content:
Scientists studying ancient microfossils from Brazil have discovered that structures once believed to be traces left behind by tiny animals were actually formed by communities of microscopic bacteria and algae. The findings challenge previous ideas about when small animals first appeared on Earth and suggest oxygen levels in ancient oceans may still have been too low to support certain forms of animal life around 540 million years ago.

The research focused on fossils found in the Brazilian state of Mato Grosso do Sul and was published in the journal Gondwana Research. Earlier studies had interpreted the marks as evidence of wormlike creatures or other tiny marine animals moving through seafloor sediment during the Ediacaran period, which came just before the Cambrian explosion.

"Using microtomography and spectroscopy techniques, we observed that the microfossils have cellular structures -- sometimes with preserved organic material -- consistent with bacteria or algae that existed during that period. These aren't traces of animals that may have passed through the area," says Bruno Becker-Kerber, the first author of the study. He carried out the research during postdoctoral work at the Institute of Geosciences at the University of São Paulo (USP) and at the Brazilian Center for Research in Energy and Materials (CNPEM), with support from FAPESP.

Becker-Kerber, who is now conducting postdoctoral research at Harvard University, explains that if the marks had truly been left by animals, they would represent evidence of meiofauna during the Ediacaran period. Meiofauna are tiny invertebrates measuring less than one millimeter long. Finding them in rocks this old would have pushed back the fossil record for these organisms significantly.

Ancient Oceans Before the Cambrian Explosion

The Ediacaran period occurred before the Cambrian explosion, a major evolutionary turning point when rising oxygen levels helped complex organisms diversify rapidly across Earth's oceans. Fossil evidence clearly shows meiofauna existed during the Cambrian, but the new findings suggest they may not have been present earlier in the way some scientists proposed.

The project forms part of the "Rio de la Plata Craton and Western Gondwana" study supported by FAPESP and coordinated by Miguel Angelo Stipp Basei, a professor at IGc-USP and coauthor of the paper.

Another coauthor, Lucas Warren of São Paulo State University (IGCE-UNESP) in Rio Claro, also received support from FAPESP.

Researchers reexamined fossils collected in Corumbá and also analyzed newly studied material from Bonito in the Serra da Bodoquena region. Both sites are located in Mato Grosso do Sul within the Tamengo geological formation.

These rocks formed in a shallow marine environment along a continental shelf during the final stages of Gondwana's formation, before the supercontinent eventually split apart to form regions that became South America and Africa.

The same research group previously identified what may be the oldest known lichen fossil, also discovered in Mato Grosso do Sul and younger than the bacteria and algae described in the current study.

High Resolution Fossil Imaging Revealed Hidden Structures

To investigate the fossils in greater detail, the team used the MOGNO beamline at Sirius, CNPEM's particle accelerator facility in Campinas. The technology allowed researchers to study fossils ranging from only a few micrometers to a few millimeters in size.

The scientists used both microtomography and nanotomography, techniques capable of generating images at extremely small scales, including micrometers (one-thousandth of a millimeter) and nanometers (one-billionth of a meter).

"When you have a large sample and want to image a structure inside it, the resolution obtained is often insufficient. The MOGNO beamline is one of the few in the world that performs so-called zoom tomography, in which we focus on something inside the sample and analyze it at the nanoscale without destroying the sample," says Becker-Kerber.

He notes that the earlier study interpreting the structures as animal traces did not have access to this level of imaging technology.

Researchers also used Raman spectroscopy to examine the fossils' chemical makeup. The technique identified organic material within fossil cell walls, strengthening the interpretation that the structures were preserved microbial bodies rather than marks left behind by passing animals.

Giant Ancient Bacteria and Algae

Some fossil samples contained pyrite, a mineral made of iron and sulfur. Based on the shapes and chemistry of the specimens, researchers believe some may represent sulfur-oxidizing bacteria, organisms that use sulfur in their metabolism.

"This group of bacteria is surprising. Some of the largest ever recorded belong precisely to this category. Unlike the common image we have of microscopic bacteria, certain species can reach diameters larger than a strand of hair and are visible to the naked eye," says Becker-Kerber.

Although the fossils do not preserve enough detail to identify exact species, the researchers found preserved cells, divisions within cell walls, and traces of organic matter across multiple collection sites. According to the team, these features would not exist if the structures were simply disturbances created by moving animals.

The fossils also appear in three different size ranges, suggesting several species may have lived together in microbial communities. The largest forms resemble green or red algae, while the smaller fossils may represent algae, cyanobacteria, or sulfur-oxidizing bacteria.

"There are concave and convex partitions, coiled filaments, cells without sediment but containing organic matter. This evidence is much closer to bacteria or algae than to mere marks of disturbance caused by animals," the researcher concludes.

The findings provide scientists with a clearer picture of the world before the Cambrian explosion and may help researchers better understand the environmental conditions that paved the way for the rise of complex animal life.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery overturning previous assumptions about early animal life, using advanced imaging technology to reveal new insights into ancient microbial life. This breakthrough has broad implications for understanding Earth's evolutionary history and environmental conditions before the Cambrian explosion, offering a meaningful contribution to science with tangible impact on knowledge about life origins.
articles.personality_title: New fossil study reveals ancient bacteria, reshaping ideas about early animal life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied tiny fossils from Brazil that are about 540 million years old. These fossils come from a time before the Cambrian explosion, when many complex animals first appeared on Earth. Previously, some marks in the fossils were thought to be traces left by tiny worm-like animals.

**What happened** – A team of researchers used advanced imaging tools to look closely at these fossils again. They found that the marks were not from animals but from communities of bacteria and algae. These microbes had well-preserved cells and even organic material inside them. The fossils were studied using special microscopes at a particle accelerator in Brazil, which allowed scientists to see details too small to see before.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists think about when small animals first appeared. Since these marks are from microbes, not animals, it suggests that the ocean’s oxygen levels back then might have been too low to support tiny animals. It also shows that life before the Cambrian explosion was mostly microbial, helping us understand Earth’s early environments better.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying similar fossils to learn more about ancient life and how oxygen and other conditions changed over time. This work might reveal more about how life on Earth evolved from simple microbes to complex animals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New fossil evidence shows ancient microbial communities, not tiny animals, lived 540 million years ago, reshaping our understanding of early life on Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude fossile révèle d’anciennes bactéries, modifiant les idées sur la vie animale précoce
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps de minuscules fossiles du Brésil âgés d’environ 540 millions d’années, datant d’une période avant l’explosion cambrienne, quand de nombreux animaux complexes sont apparus sur Terre. Auparavant, certaines marques dans ces fossiles étaient considérées comme des traces laissées par de petits animaux ressemblant à des vers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs a utilisé des outils d’imagerie avancés pour examiner ces fossiles de nouveau. Ils ont découvert que ces marques ne provenaient pas d’animaux, mais de communautés de bactéries et d’algues. Ces microbes avaient des cellules bien conservées et même du matériel organique à l’intérieur. Les fossiles ont été étudiés grâce à des microscopes spéciaux dans un accélérateur de particules au Brésil, permettant de voir des détails trop petits pour être vus auparavant.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon dont les scientifiques pensent à l’apparition des petits animaux. Puisque ces traces viennent de microbes et non d’animaux, cela suggère que le niveau d’oxygène dans les océans à cette époque était peut-être trop faible pour soutenir de petits animaux. Cela montre aussi que la vie avant l’explosion cambrienne était principalement microbienne, ce qui aide à mieux comprendre les environnements anciens de la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier des fossiles similaires pour en apprendre davantage sur la vie ancienne et comment l’oxygène et d’autres conditions ont évolué avec le temps. Ce travail pourrait révéler comment la vie sur Terre est passée de microbes simples à des animaux complexes.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – De nouvelles preuves fossiles montrent que des communautés microbiennes anciennes, et non de petits animaux, vivaient il y a 540 millions d’années, modifiant notre compréhension de la vie précoce sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio fósil revela bacterias antiguas, cambiando ideas sobre la vida animal temprana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo pequeños fósiles de Brasil que tienen unos 540 millones de años. Estos fósiles provienen de un tiempo antes de la explosión del Cámbrico, cuando aparecieron muchos animales complejos en la Tierra. Antes, algunas marcas en los fósiles se pensaba que eran rastros dejados por pequeños animales parecidos a gusanos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores usó herramientas avanzadas de imagen para mirar estos fósiles otra vez. Descubrieron que las marcas no eran de animales, sino de comunidades de bacterias y algas. Estos microbios tenían células muy bien conservadas e incluso material orgánico dentro de ellas. Los fósiles se estudiaron con microscopios especiales en un acelerador de partículas en Brasil, lo que permitió ver detalles demasiado pequeños para ver antes.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia la forma en que los científicos piensan sobre cuándo aparecieron los pequeños animales. Como estas marcas son de microbios, no de animales, sugiere que los niveles de oxígeno en el océano entonces podrían haber sido demasiado bajos para apoyar a pequeños animales. También muestra que la vida antes de la explosión del Cámbrico era principalmente microbiana, ayudándonos a entender mejor los ambientes antiguos de la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando fósiles similares para aprender más sobre la vida antigua y cómo cambiaron el oxígeno y otras condiciones con el tiempo. Este trabajo podría revelar más sobre cómo la vida en la Tierra evolucionó de microbios simples a animales complejos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevas evidencias fósiles muestran que comunidades microbianas antiguas, no pequeños animales, vivieron hace 540 millones de años, cambiando nuestra comprensión de la vida temprana en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of ancient microscopic bacterial and algal communities beneath a shallow, prehistoric ocean surface, depicted as softly glowing, cellular structures clustered together on a textured seafloor with subtle mineral crystals, illuminated by gentle light filtering through calm water, evoking a serene and mysterious early Earth environment before animal life emerged.

articles.title: How to vibe code in science: early adopters share their tips
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01477-wUsing AI coding tools can speed up your work, but there are plenty of pitfalls.
articles.content:
Last year, climate researcher Zeke Hausfather was playing around with climate-data visualizations, trying to find new and shocking ways to show just how fast Earth is warming. He was brainstorming ideas with an artificial-intelligence tool and getting it to code and create them quickly. Together, they made innovative tree-ring-style plots with the months of the year around each ring, the annual circles growing outwards with time and the colours showing temperature. Then Hausfather asked the AI tool: what if these plots were 3D?

The result was what Hausfather calls a thermal helix animation, showing temperature spiralling upwards through time into a shape reminiscent of a tornado (see ‘A new view’). In a world in which most people have seen the classic ‘hockey-stick’ graph of rising global temperatures, it is a refreshing graphic: compelling and beautiful. And, despite being a competent coder, Hausfather had no idea how to make it on his own.

Hausfather, a researcher at the climate data non-profit organization Berkeley Earth in California, is not alone in using AI tools in this way. Thanks to large language models (LLMs), people can now simply ask their computers to write and implement code for graphics, applications, data processing and just about anything else they can imagine.

This kind of laid-back, conversational technique is often called vibe coding. Andrej Karpathy, co-founder of US firm OpenAI, coined the term last year. It refers to asking an LLM-powered tool to build or do something with code behind it, with the user providing clarifying prompts until the results look right. At its purest, vibe coding doesn’t involve looking at the code — just the product. But the term has no strict definition, so what counts as vibe coding is fuzzy. Plenty of people with coding know-how start a project by vibing and then check the code by hand, or start coding by themselves and then ask an AI tool to fill in the gaps.

Credit: Zeke Hausfather

Nature spoke to a variety of scientists, from highly adept coders to complete beginners, and those in the middle, such as Hausfather, who are using AI to stretch the limits of what they can do. Many use AI-assisted coding in their work, and some are intentionally testing its limits. All of them said the AI tools that are already out there are impressive, helping them to drastically speed up their work or try out fresh ideas. But they also warn that the tools should be used with caution, and some had scary stories to tell.

All aboard

In some ways, vibe coding is the culmination of a long evolution of computer interfaces. In the 1960s, people used punch cards to communicate with machines. Computer scientists soon developed coding languages — such as BASIC and later Python — which made giving instructions to computers more natural. And developers made software systems so that non-coders could create with aplomb in limited contexts: Microsoft Word, for example, lets users make formatting changes to documents without knowing how to code. What’s new is the unparalleled speed and versatility that LLMs bring to generating code, alongside their quirky tendency to make things up and get things wrong.

What are the best AI tools for research? Nature’s guide

Although any LLM can be used to generate code, systems have emerged over the past few years that are specialized for the task, including GitHub Copilot, Anysphere’s Cursor, Anthropic’s Claude Code, Google’s Gemini Code Assist and OpenAI’s Codex. These systems can make a functional app with as little as a single-sentence prompt. The results can contain glitches, however. For example, Anthropic’s Claude Opus 4.7 is currently leading the pack on Vibe Code Bench — a benchmarking test for the functionality of web applications that have been autonomously coded by AI tools — but with an accuracy score of 71%.

Similarly to other AI products, AI coding tools are improving over time. Those that have been released in the past year or so act like friendly project managers, says Hausfather. You can feed them pages-long descriptions of goals and requirements, and they will respond with, say, a coding plan, suggested verification tests, multiple-choice options for interface design and thousands of lines of code that is well documented with explanations. The progress has impressed Hausfather: today, he says, AI code is “as bug-free as my code”.

Among professional coders in the software community, almost everyone now leans heavily on AI, vibing or otherwise. A survey this year by DX — a company in Salt Lake City, Utah, which focuses on measuring developer productivity — showed that more than 90% of software developers use AI coding assistants at least once a month, and entirely AI-authored code now makes up more than one-quarter of customer-facing code.

It’s hard to know how many researchers are jumping on the vibe-coding train, but there is clearly interest. When Argonne National Laboratory in Lemont, Illinois, set up a one-day vibe-coding hackathon last June for its researchers, it hit its capacity of 200 participants.

Manuel Corpas, a genomicist and health-data scientist at the University of Westminster in London, says there’s a real thirst for vibing in his community. He vibe-coded a project called ClawBio in two days and released it at an Imperial College London hackathon in early March. It acts as a kind of library for pieces of code that are useful in bioinformatics, such as instructions for extracting data from scientific figures, or for developing personalized medication advice on the basis of a genome sequence hosted on your computer. AI agents can pull code from the library to incorporate these ‘skills’ into their own tasks.

Following its launch, Corpas says, ClawBio racked up an impressive 5,000 downloads in just two weeks, and the community had added dozens of new skills, which were themselves vibe coded, he says.

Good news first

Rosemarie Wilton, a molecular biologist at Argonne National Laboratory, has no coding experience. But she uses established software packages to compile and analyse her data sets on viruses found in waste water, so she attended the lab’s hackathon last June to see what AI coding tools might do for her.

AI ‘scientists’ joined these research teams: here’s what happened

Wilton was impressed. She doesn’t have a graduate student, but AI tools acted like one. She could ask them to do simple tasks such as running data through one software package after another, cross-checking or producing graphs of outputs in particular ways. The AI tools could run independently all day.

To test out new data-processing pipelines, Wilton would ordinarily start each step manually or contact Argonne’s Data Science and Learning division to code the pipeline for her, but now AI can speed up this exploratory phase. If she finds an approach that works well, she says, she would ask the division to code it for her properly before, say, submitting processed data to the state health department.

As a side benefit, says Wilton, the ease of vibe coding makes it less intimidating for her to learn some coding herself. “I can learn a lot from it, not having done a lot of Python coding,” says Wilton. “It has opened up my world.”

Molecular biologists Rosemarie Wilton (right) and Sarah Owens test AI workflows at Argonne National Laboratory in Lemont, Illinois.Credit: Argonne National Laboratory

Speed and nimbleness are highlights of AI coding for everyone who Nature spoke to. Hausfather says that the code for, say, turning one axis of a graph into a log scale or layering extra information into a chart is often non-intuitive. The ability to tell his computer to do such things in plain English is, he says, “magical”. Vibe coding has also enabled him to build and host websites in a day (including a dashboard that creates constantly updated, visually appealing charts of global temperature), which he had never done before.

Tim Hobbs, a theoretical physicist at Argonne who also attended the hackathon, says he uses AI “all the time”, because coding is a big part of his job. He explores physics that goes beyond the standard model of particle physics, for example, by analysing reams of data from particle accelerators to test underlying theories or attempt to discover new ones. There are plenty of mathematical approaches he could take, so he has vibe-coded to see which ones seem more promising.

“It’s like handing off a problem to an extremely competent graduate student,” he says. “I can just quickly try a variety of ideas and maybe discard some that are suboptimal.” He says he checks the code behind anything important, such as a research paper intended for publication.

Hobbs is impressed by how cleanly AI code is written, with plenty of hand-holding annotations, often to a higher standard than the human-generated code he sees accompanying published papers. “Human code has a messiness to it, because we’re human,” he says.

For a paper1 published earlier this year, Jesse Meyer, an analytical chemist and expert in computational biomedicine at Cedars Sinai Medical Center in Los Angeles, California, attempted to demonstrate the power of AI. His team has previously developed software packages for biological data processing. This time, he used an LLM-powered app builder to vibe-code a pipeline for analysing proteomics and other ‘omics’ data.

Meet the academics refusing to use generative AI

He found that it took less than ten minutes, four well-written prompts and less than US$2 in fees to create something that might reasonably take professional coders months or even years to develop without AI. “The barrier to trying something new is very low,” Meyer says. He sees a future in which, instead of publishing code, researchers publish their prompts or ‘vibe blueprints’ for others to use.

But Meyer emphasizes that his work is just a demonstration of what can be done: he wouldn’t recommend vibe-coding anything important without substantial checks. When he published this experiment, he included a disclaimer in the paper’s introduction: “Vibe coding is not a substitute for understanding statistical analysis or computational logic.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant positive impact of AI-assisted 'vibe coding' in scientific research, enabling faster, more accessible, and innovative data analysis and visualization. This technological advancement benefits a broad range of researchers and has the potential to accelerate scientific discovery and climate action. The detailed examples and expert testimonials provide substantial context and demonstrate meaningful real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use AI ‘vibe coding’ to speed up research and create new data visuals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Scientists often need to write computer code to analyze data and create visuals. Coding can be hard and slow, especially for researchers who are not expert programmers. Recently, new AI tools can write and improve code by simply following spoken or written instructions.

**What happened**
Climate researcher Zeke Hausfather used one of these AI tools to create a new 3D animation showing rising temperatures over time. This kind of coding by conversation is called “vibe coding.” Many scientists, from beginners to experts, are now using AI to help write code faster and try new ideas. At a recent event in Illinois, 200 researchers joined a vibe-coding hackathon to explore these tools. For example, molecular biologist Rosemarie Wilton found AI helped her run complex data analyses without needing to code herself.

**Impact**
Vibe coding allows scientists to work faster and explore new ideas without deep coding skills. It makes coding less intimidating and helps researchers create better graphs and apps. Some AI tools can write thousands of lines of code with explanations, and many professional coders now rely on AI assistants regularly. This means research can move more quickly, and scientists can focus more on discoveries than on programming details.

**What's next step**
Researchers plan to keep testing and improving AI coding tools. Some hope to share their “vibe blueprints,” or instructions they give AI, so others can reuse them. However, scientists agree AI-generated code must be carefully checked before being used in important work. As AI tools get better, more scientists may adopt vibe coding to speed up projects and create new ways to understand data.

**One-sentence takeaway**
AI-powered vibe coding is helping scientists write code faster and explore new ideas, making research more accessible and efficient.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques utilisent le « vibe coding » IA pour accélérer la recherche et créer de nouvelles visualisations de données
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Les scientifiques doivent souvent écrire du code informatique pour analyser des données et créer des images. Le codage peut être difficile et lent, surtout pour ceux qui ne sont pas experts. Récemment, de nouveaux outils d’IA peuvent écrire et améliorer le code en suivant simplement des instructions écrites ou orales.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Le chercheur en climatologie Zeke Hausfather a utilisé un de ces outils d’IA pour créer une nouvelle animation 3D montrant la montée des températures au fil du temps. Ce type de codage par conversation s’appelle le « vibe coding ». De nombreux scientifiques, débutants comme experts, utilisent maintenant l’IA pour écrire du code plus rapidement et tester de nouvelles idées. Lors d’un récent hackathon dans l’Illinois, 200 chercheurs ont exploré ces outils. Par exemple, la biologiste moléculaire Rosemarie Wilton a pu analyser des données complexes sans coder elle-même grâce à l’IA.

**Impact**
Le vibe coding permet aux scientifiques de travailler plus vite et d’explorer de nouvelles idées sans être experts en codage. Cela rend le codage moins intimidant et aide à créer de meilleurs graphiques et applications. Certains outils d’IA peuvent écrire des milliers de lignes de code avec des explications, et de nombreux programmeurs professionnels utilisent maintenant régulièrement ces assistants. La recherche peut ainsi avancer plus rapidement, en se concentrant davantage sur les découvertes que sur la programmation.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs vont continuer à tester et améliorer ces outils d’IA. Certains souhaitent partager leurs « plans de vibe coding », c’est-à-dire les instructions données à l’IA, pour que d’autres puissent les réutiliser. Cependant, ils insistent sur le fait que le code généré par l’IA doit être vérifié avant toute utilisation importante. Avec l’amélioration des outils, de plus en plus de scientifiques adopteront le vibe coding pour accélérer leurs projets et mieux comprendre les données.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Le vibe coding assisté par IA aide les scientifiques à écrire du code plus rapidement et à explorer de nouvelles idées, rendant la recherche plus accessible et efficace.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos usan ‘vibe coding’ con IA para acelerar la investigación y crear nuevas visualizaciones de datos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Los científicos a menudo necesitan escribir código para analizar datos y crear gráficos. Programar puede ser difícil y lento, especialmente para quienes no son expertos. Recientemente, nuevas herramientas de IA pueden escribir y mejorar código siguiendo instrucciones habladas o escritas.

**Qué pasó**
El investigador climático Zeke Hausfather usó una de estas herramientas de IA para crear una nueva animación 3D que muestra el aumento de las temperaturas con el tiempo. Este tipo de programación por conversación se llama “vibe coding”. Muchos científicos, desde principiantes hasta expertos, usan IA para escribir código más rápido y probar nuevas ideas. En un reciente hackathon en Illinois, 200 investigadores exploraron estas herramientas. Por ejemplo, la bióloga molecular Rosemarie Wilton pudo analizar datos complejos sin necesidad de programar gracias a la IA.

**Impacto**
El vibe coding permite a los científicos trabajar más rápido y explorar nuevas ideas sin ser expertos en programación. Hace que programar sea menos intimidante y ayuda a crear mejores gráficos y aplicaciones. Algunas herramientas de IA pueden escribir miles de líneas de código con explicaciones, y muchos programadores profesionales ahora usan asistentes de IA regularmente. Esto permite que la investigación avance más rápido, enfocándose más en los descubrimientos que en la programación.

**Próximo paso**
Los investigadores planean seguir probando y mejorando las herramientas de IA. Algunos quieren compartir sus “planos de vibe coding”, o instrucciones que dan a la IA, para que otros puedan usarlos. Sin embargo, coinciden en que el código generado por IA debe revisarse cuidadosamente antes de usarse en trabajos importantes. A medida que las herramientas mejoren, más científicos adoptarán el vibe coding para acelerar proyectos y entender mejor los datos.

**Resumen en una frase**
El vibe coding con IA ayuda a los científicos a escribir código más rápido y explorar nuevas ideas, haciendo la investigación más accesible y eficiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, spiraling helix made of vibrant, flowing lines symbolizing rising temperatures and AI-generated code, intertwining with abstract representations of data streams and digital tools, set against a soft, natural-toned background that evokes innovation and collaboration in scientific discovery.

articles.title: NASA Langley Engineer Attends FAA Training
articles.summary: At a busy airport, every aircraft in the area shares just a handful of radio frequencies. Spectrum and time are constrained and if multiple people speak at once, both messages can get lost. Communications like “clearance delivery,” which require long transmissions and readbacks, are challenging in high-traffic areas, particularly when weather or other factors require […]
articles.content:
At a busy airport, every aircraft in the area shares just a handful of radio frequencies. Spectrum and time are constrained and if multiple people speak at once, both messages can get lost. Communications like “clearance delivery,” which require long transmissions and readbacks, are challenging in high-traffic areas, particularly when weather or other factors require many aircraft to communicate with controllers at once. Going digital clears that channel for urgent, time-critical calls, among other things. And it’s the current practice at some airports, where pilots can confirm clearances with the touch of a button, that the response goes directly to the controller’s screen, and the updated information loads into their flight management system. Will Cummings-Grande, aerospace engineer with the Systems Analysis and Concepts Directorate based at NASA’s Langley Research Center, is leading technical work that centers around Communications Architecture and Performance for Digital Clearance in NASA’s Air Traffic Management and Safety (ATMS) project. He’s researching the next layer of digital clearance, extending that same logic down to taxi instructions on the ground, so that pushback timing, routing, and runway assignments could also arrive digitally rather than over the radio. He sought out the most current, ground-level knowledge about how digital clearance delivery works in practice — not in a research paper, but in a real tower, on real systems, with the people who run them every day. The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) offers the training he wanted to air traffic controllers, so he reached out to the FAA Academy “on a hope and a prayer” that they might accept him as a student. And in early April, Cummings-Grande traveled to the Mike Monroney Aeronautical Center (MMAC) in Oklahoma City to complete the Tower Data Link Services (TDLS) Application Specialist training — the same two-day, hands-on course required of working controllers at the 72 U.S. airports currently equipped with digital clearance delivery capability. Will Cummings-Grande, aerospace engineer with the Systems Analysis and Concepts Directorate, based at NASA’s Langley Research Center Credit: NASA

The air traffic control tower at the Mike Monroney Aeronautical Center in Oklahoma City, where Cummings-Grande visited to observe the Tower Data Link Services system in live operation. Credit: Will Cummings-Grande/NASA

In the Classroom

Cumming-Grande shadowed a working controller during exercises, trading off at the terminal during breaks so both got time on the system. His classmates were application specialists from Seattle, Sacramento, San Jose, and Fort Lauderdale, all controllers with day jobs managing high-traffic airspace who were there to become the designated system maintainers at their home airports. During breaks, Cummings-Grande had a luxury: time to test. “I got to bounce some of my ideas and concepts off of controllers who are out there interacting with the TDLS and all of the tools it touches in the current system,” he said. “It was great to have both — here’s what the controller-in-training gets, and here’s what I get as a researcher — kind of lumped into the same experience.”

The FAA Academy also connected him with the systems engineers responsible for developing, testing, and implementing new TDLS hardware and software versions, and arranged a visit to the OKC tower to observe the system in live operation.

What He Found

The TDLS runs on fully air-gapped software, completely isolated from standard operating systems — a deliberate cybersecurity design that made the hands-on experience revelatory in ways a research paper couldn’t replicate. “Interacting with the system was just very eye-opening as to how different these systems are from other computers that we commonly interact with,” he said.

The more significant discovery came from the curriculum itself. Reviewing the FAA’s system architecture during training, Cummings-Grande noticed something he didn’t know to look for: a link between the TDLS and the Terminal Flight Data Manager (TFDM), which does not yet exist operationally. That gap is now the center of his research questions. “I didn’t realize I was missing this piece until I took this course,” he said.

Building on Two Decades of Homework

The research Cummings-Grande is pursuing connects to a long thread of NASA work on surface safety and digital communications, including the Terminal Area Productivity program, the Surface Operation Automation Research (SOAR) project, the Low Visibility Landing and Surface Operations (LVLASO) project, and Surface Trajectory Based Operations (STBO) studies. These efforts kicked off in the mid-90s to inform FAA NextGen and demonstrated digital taxi clearances in a series of simulations at multiple facilities and ultimately flight tests at the Atlanta Airport. Those findings showed meaningful workload reductions, but the cost-benefit case wasn’t there yet, and the technology wasn’t ready in the fleet or in the facilities.

What’s changed, in Cummings-Grande’s view, is the convergence of new infrastructure investments, including the rollout of systems derived from Airspace Technology Demonstration (ATD-2) technologies like the Spot and Runway Departure Advisor and the Precision Departure Release Capability through the TFDM, with renewed industry interest from a partner on the aircraft side. “We have all this homework that people have been doing for the last 20-30 years,” he said. “Can we take advantage of the renewed interest from FAA and industry to enable this safety-enhancement?”

His timeline estimate for a fully implemented system leans somewhere in the range of five to ten years. And the payoff, he says, will be tangible to anyone who flies. “This means that your flight will be safer than ever, and that your pilots will be focused on the right things during taxi. Instead of relying on pilots to write down their taxi clearance correctly or be familiar with the airport, the airplane will know and can double-check what the pilot is doing.”

A Case for Partnership

Cummings-Grande isn’t aware of another NASA researcher having taken this FAA course, and he thinks the model is worth repeating. He pointed to terminal procedures design (TERPS) as another area where FAA Academy training could benefit researchers working on urban air mobility and small UAS integration. “Anytime someone needs to do a deep dive into one of the systems — understanding the current state of practice, here are the buttons you push to make this happen — I think it’d be great to have an ongoing partnership with the FAA Academy and make that possible.”

The FAA Academy team was, by all accounts, a willing partner.

Cummings-Grande extends his special thanks to the FAA’s Eric Gandrud and Carol Raiford.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant advancement in digital air traffic communication technology led by a NASA engineer collaborating with the FAA. This innovation promises to improve aviation safety and efficiency on a broad scale, benefiting all air travelers. The story provides detailed context about the research, training, and potential impact, making it a substantive and meaningful positive development in air traffic management technology.
articles.personality_title: NASA Engineer Trains with FAA to Improve Digital Airport Communications
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Airports have limited radio channels for communication, which can cause problems when many planes try to talk to air traffic controllers at the same time. This is especially hard during busy or bad weather conditions. To fix this, some airports use digital systems that let pilots and controllers send messages directly through computers instead of radios.

**What happened** – Will Cummings-Grande, an aerospace engineer at NASA’s Langley Research Center, took a special two-day training course at the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Academy in Oklahoma City. This course teaches how to use the Tower Data Link Services (TDLS), a digital system used by air traffic controllers at 72 U.S. airports. He worked alongside real controllers and learned how the system operates in real life.

**Impact** – This training helped Cummings-Grande discover new ways to improve digital clearance, like sending taxi instructions to planes digitally. This can reduce mistakes and keep pilots more focused during ground movements. His work builds on decades of NASA research and could make flying safer and smoother by connecting more airport systems together.

**What's next step** – Cummings-Grande will continue researching how to link digital clearance systems with other flight data tools to create a fully digital process for guiding planes on the ground. He expects it could take 5 to 10 years to put this system into full use, benefiting passengers by making flights safer and more efficient.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A NASA engineer’s hands-on training with the FAA is helping develop better digital communication systems that could make airport operations safer and easier for pilots and passengers alike.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un ingénieur de la NASA suit une formation FAA pour améliorer les communications numériques dans les aéroports
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les aéroports disposent de peu de fréquences radio, ce qui complique la communication lorsque plusieurs avions parlent en même temps, surtout par mauvais temps ou en période de forte activité. Pour résoudre ce problème, certains aéroports utilisent des systèmes numériques qui permettent aux pilotes et aux contrôleurs de s’envoyer des messages directement via des ordinateurs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Will Cummings-Grande, ingénieur aérospatial au Centre de recherche Langley de la NASA, a suivi une formation pratique de deux jours à l’Académie de la Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) à Oklahoma City. Cette formation porte sur les Tower Data Link Services (TDLS), un système numérique utilisé par les contrôleurs dans 72 aéroports américains. Il a travaillé avec de vrais contrôleurs pour apprendre le fonctionnement réel du système.

**Impact** – Cette formation a permis à Cummings-Grande de découvrir de nouvelles façons d’améliorer la délivrance numérique des autorisations, notamment pour envoyer des instructions de roulage aux avions par voie numérique. Cela peut réduire les erreurs et aider les pilotes à mieux se concentrer au sol. Ses travaux s’appuient sur des recherches NASA de longue date et pourraient rendre les vols plus sûrs et plus fluides en connectant davantage de systèmes aéroportuaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Cummings-Grande va poursuivre ses recherches pour relier les systèmes de délivrance numérique aux autres outils de gestion des données de vol afin de créer un processus entièrement numérique pour guider les avions au sol. Il estime que la mise en place complète pourrait prendre entre 5 et 10 ans, au bénéfice des passagers grâce à des vols plus sûrs et plus efficaces.

**En une phrase** – La formation pratique d’un ingénieur NASA avec la FAA aide à développer des systèmes de communication numérique améliorés qui pourraient rendre les opérations aéroportuaires plus sûres et plus simples pour les pilotes et les passagers.
articles.personality_title_es: Ingeniero de la NASA se capacita con la FAA para mejorar las comunicaciones digitales en aeropuertos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los aeropuertos tienen pocas frecuencias de radio, lo que dificulta la comunicación cuando muchos aviones hablan al mismo tiempo, especialmente en condiciones climáticas adversas o con mucho tráfico. Para solucionar esto, algunos aeropuertos usan sistemas digitales que permiten a pilotos y controladores enviarse mensajes directamente a través de computadoras.

**Qué pasó** – Will Cummings-Grande, ingeniero aeroespacial del Centro de Investigación Langley de la NASA, tomó un curso práctico de dos días en la Academia de la Administración Federal de Aviación (FAA) en Oklahoma City. Este curso enseña a usar los Tower Data Link Services (TDLS), un sistema digital utilizado por controladores en 72 aeropuertos de EE.UU. Trabajó junto a controladores reales para aprender cómo funciona el sistema en la práctica.

**Impacto** – Esta capacitación ayudó a Cummings-Grande a descubrir nuevas formas de mejorar la autorización digital, como enviar instrucciones de rodaje a los aviones por medios digitales. Esto puede reducir errores y ayudar a los pilotos a concentrarse mejor en tierra. Su trabajo se basa en décadas de investigación de la NASA y podría hacer que volar sea más seguro y fluido al conectar más sistemas del aeropuerto.

**Próximo paso** – Cummings-Grande seguirá investigando cómo enlazar los sistemas de autorización digital con otras herramientas de datos de vuelo para crear un proceso completamente digital para guiar a los aviones en tierra. Calcula que podría tomar entre 5 y 10 años implementar el sistema completo, beneficiando a los pasajeros con vuelos más seguros y eficientes.

**Resumen en una frase** – La capacitación práctica de un ingeniero de la NASA con la FAA está ayudando a desarrollar mejores sistemas de comunicación digital que podrían hacer las operaciones aeroportuarias más seguras y fáciles para pilotos y pasajeros.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warmly lit air traffic control tower silhouette overlooks a bustling airport runway, with stylized digital connection lines flowing seamlessly between the tower and multiple airplanes on the ground and in the sky, symbolizing advanced digital communication and collaboration between engineers and controllers enhancing flight safety.
